questionText: Complete the sentence: My sister wants to be ______ astronaut when she grows up.
explanation: We use 'an' before words that start with a vowel sound (a, e, i, o, u). 'Astronaut' starts with a vowel sound, so the correct article is 'an'.

questionText: ______ students in my class passed the English exam because it was very difficult.
explanation: 'A few' is used with countable nouns (students) to mean a small number. The sentence indicates the exam was difficult, so only a small number passed. 'A little' is for uncountable nouns. 'Many' is also for countable nouns but implies a large number, which doesn't fit the context.

questionText: I have ______ money left, so I can't buy that new novel.
explanation: 'No' means 'not any'. The sentence says the person cannot buy the novel, so they have zero money. 'Many' and 'a few' are for countable nouns (money is uncountable). 'Some' is positive, meaning an amount exists.

questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: There isn't some water in the bottle.
There is any water in the bottle.
There is no water in the bottle.
There are no water in the bottle.
explanation: 'No' is used with a positive verb to mean 'not any'. 'Water' is uncountable, so the verb must be singular ('is'). Option C is correct. 'Some' is not used in negative sentences (Option A is wrong). 'Any' is used in questions/negatives but not alone with a positive verb (Option B is wrong). Option D uses the plural verb 'are' with an uncountable noun.

questionText: We have two options for the project. ______ option is acceptable to the teacher, so we can choose either one.
explanation: 'Either' means 'one or the other of two'. Since both options are acceptable, 'either' works. 'Neither' means 'not one nor the other', which is incorrect here. 'Both' would require a plural verb ('are acceptable').

questionText: My mother gave me ______ advice on how to study for the BEPC exam. It was very useful.
explanation: 'Advice' is an uncountable noun. 'A little' is used with uncountable nouns to mean a small but positive amount. 'Many' and 'a few' are for countable nouns. 'Few' (without 'a') has a negative meaning (almost none), which doesn't fit 'useful'.

questionText: ______ of the two candidates is qualified for the position of class president. They are both excellent.
explanation: 'Either' means 'one or the other'. The sentence says both are excellent, so choosing either one is fine. 'Both' would require 'are' (Both are qualified). 'Neither' means not one, which is false. 'None' is for three or more items.

questionText: How ______ sugar do you want in your tea?
explanation: 'Sugar' is an uncountable noun. We use 'much' with uncountable nouns in questions and negative sentences. 'How much' asks about quantity. 'How many' is for countable nouns.

questionText: I have read ______ books about climate change, but I need to read more for my presentation.
explanation: 'Books' is a countable noun. 'A few' means a small number, which fits the idea that the person has read some but needs more. 'A little' is for uncountable nouns. 'Much' is usually for questions/negatives with uncountable nouns. 'Any' is for questions/negatives.

questionText: Complete the sentence with the correct quantifier: 'There are ______ people in the market today because of the heavy rain.'
explanation: 'People' is a countable plural noun. 'Very few' means almost none. Heavy rain would keep people away, so very few people come. 'A great deal of' and 'much' are for uncountable nouns. 'A little' is also for uncountable nouns.

questionText: The hospital has ______ beds available for new patients. They only have two left.
explanation: 'Few' (without 'a') means 'not many' and has a negative idea (almost none). With only two beds left, the number is very small. 'A few' would mean some (positive), but 'few' emphasizes the lack. 'Beds' is countable, so 'a little' and 'much' are incorrect.

questionText: We need to buy ______ rice and ______ oranges for the family dinner.
explanation: 'Some' is used in positive sentences with both uncountable nouns (rice) and plural countable nouns (oranges). 'Any' is for questions/negatives. 'A few' is for countable (oranges) but 'a little' for uncountable (rice) — the order is wrong in option C. 'Much/many' are often used in questions/negatives, not simple positive statements like this.

questionText: I don't have ______ homework today, so I can go to football practice.
explanation: 'Homework' is an uncountable noun. In negative sentences, we use 'much' with uncountable nouns. 'Many' is for countable nouns. 'A few' and 'some' are generally used in positive sentences.

questionText: ______ my parents work in Yaoundé. My mother is a teacher and my father is an engineer.
explanation: 'Both of' is used before plural nouns/pronouns to refer to two people/things together. The sentence gives information about the mother and the father, so both work. 'Either' means one or the other. 'Neither' means not one. 'Each of' focuses on individuals, but 'both of' is more natural here.

questionText: He has waited for you for ______ hours. Please go and meet him.
explanation: 'Several' means more than two but not many, and it is used with plural countable nouns ('hours'). 'A little' and 'a great deal of' are for uncountable nouns. 'Much' is rarely used in positive statements like this (usually 'many hours' or 'several hours').

questionText: I have two pens. You can borrow ______ of them. Just choose the color you like.
explanation: 'Either' means 'one of two'. The speaker allows the person to choose one pen. 'Neither' would mean no pens. 'Both' would mean two pens, but the speaker might need one. 'None' is for three or more.

questionText: There is ______ hope for the endangered species if we continue to destroy their habitat.
explanation: 'Little' (without 'a') means 'almost none' and has a negative meaning. If we destroy habitat, there is almost no hope. 'Hope' is uncountable, so 'few' and 'a few' are incorrect. 'A little' means a small but positive amount (some hope), which does not fit the pessimistic context.

questionText: The government says ______ citizen has the right to vote in the upcoming election.
explanation: 'Every' is used with singular countable nouns ('citizen') to mean 'all members of a group'. 'All' is used with plural nouns ('all citizens'). 'Both' is for two. 'Neither' is negative.

questionText: We have ______ time before the exam starts, so let's review the phrasal verbs one more time.
explanation: 'Time' is an uncountable noun. 'A little' means a small but positive amount. The sentence suggests there is some time to review. 'A few' and 'many' are for countable nouns. 'Any' is usually for questions/negatives.

questionText: The headmaster invited ______ the students and their parents to the end-of-year ceremony.
explanation: 'All of' is used with plural nouns to refer to a group of more than two. There are many students and many parents. 'Both of' is only for two. 'Either/neither of' are for two items only and imply a choice or negation.
